refashioned:
After these topics, we should probably turn next to pleasure. Pleasure seems to be deeply bound up with human nature itself, which is precisely why we guide the young by means of pleasure and pain, as if by rudders. Moreover, taking pleasure in the right things and feeling aversion to the wrong ones is thought to matter enormously for developing virtue of character. These responses to pleasure and pain run through our entire lives, carrying great weight and influence over both virtue and the happy life, since people choose what is pleasant and avoid what is painful. It would seem, then, that we should hardly pass over such matters in silence, especially since they are subject to considerable disagreement.

Some thinkers claim that pleasure is the good, while others insist, on the contrary, that it is thoroughly bad. Some of the latter are probably genuinely convinced of this, while others merely think it better for how we live to portray pleasure as bad even if it is not, on the grounds that most people lean toward pleasure and are enslaved by their pleasures, and so ought to be pushed in the opposite direction to arrive at the mean. But surely this approach is mistaken. Arguments about matters involving feelings and actions are less convincing than the facts themselves. When such arguments conflict with what we actually perceive, they are dismissed and end up discrediting the truth along with themselves. If someone who denounces pleasure is then seen pursuing it, his inclination toward it is taken to mean that all pleasure is worth pursuing, since most people are poor at making fine distinctions.

True arguments, then, seem most useful not only for knowledge but also for life. Because they harmonize with the facts, they are believed, and thus they encourage those who understand them to live accordingly. Enough of these preliminary remarks; let us now review the opinions that have been put forward about pleasure.
elegant:
Pleasure is deeply intertwined with human nature, which is why we educate the young through pleasure and pain, and why enjoying the right things and hating the wrong ones is crucial for virtue. Since people choose what is pleasant and avoid what is painful, these matters deserve serious examination despite the controversy surrounding them.

Some say pleasure is the good; others say it is thoroughly bad, whether from conviction or from a pedagogical strategy of pushing people away from their natural excess. But this corrective approach is mistaken, for arguments that clash with observable facts are dismissed and discredit the truth itself. If a critic of pleasure is caught pursuing it, people conclude all pleasure must be worthwhile. True arguments, those that harmonize with facts, are the most useful for both knowledge and life, since they earn trust and inspire people to live accordingly.
summary: Pleasure deserves careful examination because it is central to human nature, moral education, and the pursuit of happiness. Both those who say pleasure is the supreme good and those who call it entirely bad are mistaken in different ways, and arguments about pleasure must be tested against observable facts to be credible and useful.
distilled: Pleasure is so central to human life and moral development that it demands careful, honest analysis rather than either uncritical embrace or strategic denunciation.
notes: This passage reopens the discussion of pleasure begun in Book VII (chapters 11-14), now with greater precision. Aristotle's methodological remark about arguments harmonizing with facts reflects his broader empirical commitments (cf. De Caelo I.3). -- The strategy of denouncing pleasure to correct popular excess is attributed to unnamed thinkers, possibly Speusippus or Cynics. -- The pedagogical use of pleasure and pain ("rudders") anticipates the discussion of moral habituation in X.9 and connects to II.3, where Aristotle first established pleasure and pain as central to moral training. -- The principle that theory must answer to observed phenomena (phainomena) is a hallmark of Aristotle's method, discussed in detail in VII.1.

refashioned:
Eudoxus held that pleasure was the good because he observed that all things, both rational and non-rational, aim at it, and because in every case the object of choice is what is excellent, and the most chosen thing is the greatest good. The fact that everything moves toward the same object, he argued, shows that this is the chief good for all things, since each thing finds its own good just as it finds its own nourishment. His arguments carried weight more because of the excellence of his character than because of their intrinsic force: he was reputed to be remarkably self-controlled, and so people believed he was not speaking as a partisan of pleasure, but that the facts truly were as he described.

He also believed the same conclusion followed from considering the contrary of pleasure. Pain is in itself something all things avoid, and therefore its contrary must be something all things choose. Furthermore, that is most choiceworthy which we choose not for the sake of anything else, and pleasure is admittedly of this nature, since no one asks to what end he is pleased, implying that pleasure is choiceworthy in itself. Finally, he argued that pleasure, when added to any good — for example, to just or temperate action — makes it more worthy of choice, and that the good can only be increased by itself.
elegant: Eudoxus argued that pleasure is the good on three grounds: all beings, rational and non-rational alike, aim at it; it is chosen for its own sake, since no one asks why they take pleasure; and adding pleasure to any good makes that good more choiceworthy. His arguments gained credibility from his exceptional self-discipline — people trusted he spoke from conviction about the facts, not from personal indulgence.
summary: Eudoxus argued that pleasure is the supreme good because all creatures pursue it, it is desired for its own sake rather than as a means to something else, and it enhances every good to which it is added. His well-known self-control lent his arguments special credibility.
distilled: Eudoxus's case that pleasure is the good rests on its universality as an object of pursuit, its being chosen for its own sake, and its power to enhance every other good.
notes: Eudoxus of Cnidus (c. 390-340 BCE) was a distinguished mathematician and astronomer who studied at Plato's Academy. He is credited with the theory of homocentric spheres in astronomy and the method of exhaustion in mathematics. -- His hedonistic ethics was unusual for an Academic philosopher, and Aristotle's note that his character lent his arguments credibility is significant: it suggests that in ethical argument, the ethos of the speaker matters. -- The argument that what all things aim at is the good echoes the opening of the Nicomachean Ethics (I.1). -- The final argument (that the good can be increased only by itself) is the one Plato deploys against Eudoxus in the Philebus (20c-22b), as Aristotle notes in the next passage.

refashioned:
This last argument, however, seems only to show that pleasure is one among the goods, and no more a good than any other. For every good becomes more choiceworthy when combined with another good than when taken alone. In fact, it is by precisely this kind of reasoning that Plato argues that pleasure is not the good: he contends that the pleasant life is more desirable with wisdom than without, and that if the mixture is better than pleasure alone, then pleasure cannot be the good, since the good cannot become more desirable by the addition of anything to it.

Now it is clear that nothing else can be the good either, if it too becomes more desirable when something intrinsically good is added to it. What, then, satisfies this criterion while also being something in which we can share? That is what we are looking for.

As for those who object that what all things aim at is not necessarily good, they are, we may suppose, talking nonsense. What everyone believes really is so, and anyone who attacks this conviction will hardly have anything more credible to offer in its place. If only mindless creatures desired these things, there might be something to the objection. But when rational beings desire them too, what force can the objection have? Perhaps even in lower creatures there is some natural good, something stronger than the creatures themselves, that aims at their proper good.
elegant:
Eudoxus's argument that pleasure enhances any good actually proves only that pleasure is one good among many, not the good. Plato uses the same reasoning: since a pleasant life combined with wisdom is better than pleasure alone, pleasure cannot be the supreme good — for the supreme good cannot be improved by additions. Yet nothing else passes this test either, so the question of what the good is remains open.

The objection that universal pursuit does not prove something good is weak: what everyone believes carries real weight, and the objector has nothing more credible to offer. Even non-rational creatures may possess a natural orientation toward their proper good.
summary: Eudoxus's argument that pleasure enhances other goods actually proves it is merely one good among many, not the supreme good, since the supreme good cannot be improved by additions. Plato exploits this same logic. Meanwhile, the universal pursuit of pleasure by all creatures — rational and non-rational alike — is strong evidence that it is genuinely good, even if not the highest good.
distilled: Pleasure is genuinely a good, since all creatures pursue it, but the fact that it can be enhanced by combining it with wisdom shows it cannot be the supreme good.
notes: Plato's argument appears in the Philebus (20c-22b), where Socrates argues that neither pure pleasure nor pure intellect constitutes the good life, but rather a mixture of both. -- The principle that the good cannot be made more desirable by additions is a key criterion throughout ancient ethics. -- Aristotle's defense of common opinion (endoxa) — that what all believe is so — is methodologically significant and connects to his dialectical method in the Topics. -- Speusippus, Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy, held that pleasure was neither good nor bad, which Aristotle critiques in the next passage.

refashioned: Nor does the argument about the contrary of pleasure seem correct. Some say that even if pain is an evil, it does not follow that pleasure is a good, since one evil can be opposed to another evil, and both can also be opposed to a neutral state. This is logically correct as a general point, but it does not apply to the case at hand. For if both pleasure and pain belonged to the class of evils, they would both be things to avoid. And if they both belonged to the class of neutrals, neither should be avoided, or both should be avoided equally. But in fact people evidently shun pain as an evil and choose pleasure as a good. That, then, must be the real nature of the opposition between them.
elegant: The argument that pleasure need not be good even if pain is evil — because two evils can be opposed, and both can be opposed to a neutral — is logically valid in the abstract but fails when applied to pleasure and pain. If both were evils, both would be avoided. If both were neutrals, neither would be. In reality, people shun pain as evil and choose pleasure as good, revealing the true nature of their opposition.
summary: The claim that pleasure is not necessarily good even though pain is evil rests on abstract logic about opposites but fails in practice: people consistently avoid pain as bad and choose pleasure as good, which reveals that they are genuinely opposed as evil to good.
distilled: The real opposition between pleasure and pain is not evil-to-evil or neutral-to-neutral, but evil-to-good, as shown by how people actually experience and respond to them.
notes: This argument is directed against Speusippus (c. 408-339 BCE), Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy, who held that both pleasure and pain were evils, with the good being a neutral state. -- Aristotle's response draws on his characteristic method of testing logical arguments against observed human behavior. -- The logical point about contraries and intermediates draws on the Categories (chs. 10-11) where Aristotle discusses types of opposition. -- This passage also revisits the treatment of pleasure's relationship to pain in VII.11-14.

refashioned:
Nor again does it follow that pleasure is not a good because it is not a quality. The activities of virtue are not qualities either, nor is happiness. Some say the good is determinate while pleasure is indeterminate because it admits of degrees. But if their judgment rests on the feeling of pleasure, the same will hold true of justice and the other virtues, in regard to which we readily say that people have them to a greater or lesser degree and act more or less in accordance with them. People can be more just or more brave, and it is possible to act justly or temperately more or less.

But if their judgment is based on the different kinds of pleasure, they are surely not identifying the real cause — not if some pleasures are unmixed and others are mixed. Moreover, just as health admits of degrees without being indeterminate, why should pleasure not be the same? The same proportion is not found in all things, nor does a single proportion always persist in the same thing, but it can relax and still continue up to a point, and it can differ in degree. The case of pleasure may therefore be of this kind as well.
elegant: Pleasure is not disqualified as a good simply because it is not a quality — the activities of virtue and happiness are not qualities either. Nor is it disqualified for admitting of degrees, since justice, courage, and all the virtues also admit of degrees. Just as health varies in degree without being indeterminate, so too can pleasure vary while remaining a genuine good.
summary: Two objections against pleasure being a good — that it is not a quality and that it is indeterminate because it admits of degrees — both fail. Virtue and happiness are not qualities either, and virtues like justice and courage also come in degrees. Health provides a clear analogy: it varies in degree without being indeterminate.
distilled: Pleasure's admitting of degrees no more disqualifies it as a good than the fact that justice, courage, and health also come in degrees.
notes: The objection that pleasure is not a quality likely comes from Platonic or Academic critics drawing on the Categories. -- The claim that the good must be determinate while pleasure is indeterminate reflects Plato's association of the good with limit (peras) and pleasure with the unlimited (apeiron) in the Philebus (23c-27c). -- The distinction between mixed and unmixed pleasures also echoes the Philebus (50e-53c). -- Aristotle's analogy with health anticipates his broader use of health as a model for understanding variable states throughout his ethical and biological works.

refashioned:
Again, some assume that the good is perfect and complete while processes of motion and coming-into-being are imperfect, and they try to show that pleasure is a kind of motion or coming-into-being. But they do not seem to be right even in calling it a motion. Speed and slowness are thought to belong to every motion, and if a particular motion — such as the revolution of the heavens — does not have speed or slowness in itself, it has them in relation to something else. But neither of these applies to pleasure. We can become pleased quickly, just as we can become angry quickly, but we cannot be pleased quickly — not even relative to someone else — though we can walk, grow, or do similar things quickly. The transition into a state of pleasure can happen quickly or slowly, but the actual activity of being pleased cannot.

Moreover, how can pleasure be a coming-into-being? It is not thought that any random thing comes into being from any random thing; rather, a thing dissolves back into whatever it came into being from. If pleasure were a coming-into-being, then pain would have to be the destruction of whatever pleasure is the coming-into-being of.
elegant: Those who classify pleasure as a motion or a coming-into-being, on the grounds that these are imperfect while the good must be perfect, are mistaken. Motion involves speed and slowness, but while we can become pleased quickly, we cannot be pleased quickly — the activity of pleasure itself does not admit of speed. Nor can pleasure be a coming-into-being, since that would require pain to be the destruction of whatever pleasure brings into existence.
summary: Pleasure cannot be classified as a motion or a process of coming-into-being. Motions involve speed and slowness, but the activity of being pleased does not. And if pleasure were a process of becoming, pain would have to be the corresponding destruction, which does not fit the facts.
distilled: Pleasure is neither a motion nor a process of becoming, since it lacks the defining features of both — speed and slowness in the one case, a correlative destruction in the other.
notes: The view that pleasure is a motion (kinesis) or coming-into-being (genesis) derives from Plato's Philebus (53c-55a) and possibly from some Academy members. -- Aristotle's discussion of motion draws on his precise analysis in the Physics (Books III and V), where he distinguishes kinesis from energeia (activity). -- The distinction between becoming pleased (a transition) and being pleased (an activity) is crucial to Aristotle's positive account of pleasure in X.4. -- The example of celestial motion reflects Aristotle's cosmology in De Caelo.

refashioned:
Some also say that pain is the lack of what accords with nature, and that pleasure is the replenishment of that lack. But these are bodily experiences. If pleasure is replenishment of what accords with nature, then whatever feels pleasure must be the thing being replenished — that is, the body. But this is not thought to be right. Therefore replenishment is not the same as pleasure, though someone might feel pleased while replenishment is occurring, just as someone might feel pain while being operated on.

This view seems to rest on the pains and pleasures connected with eating and drinking — the fact that when people have gone without food and suffered from the lack, they are pleased by being fed. But this does not happen with all pleasures. The pleasures of learning, for instance, involve no prior pain, and neither do many sensory pleasures — those of smell, many sounds and sights, and also memories and hopes. Of what, then, are these the coming-into-being? There was no prior lack of anything for them to be the replenishing of.
elegant: The theory that pleasure is the replenishment of a natural lack works only for certain bodily pleasures like eating after hunger. It cannot account for the pleasures of learning, the enjoyment of fragrances, beautiful sounds and sights, or the delight of memory and hope — none of which presuppose any prior deficiency that needs filling.
summary: The view that pleasure is the replenishment of a natural deficiency fails because many pleasures — such as learning, perceiving beautiful sights, sounds, and smells, and enjoying memories and hopes — do not involve any prior lack or pain that requires restoration.
distilled: Pleasure cannot be mere replenishment of a lack, since the pleasures of learning, perception, memory, and hope arise without any prior deficiency.
notes: The replenishment (anaplerosis) theory of pleasure derives from Plato's discussions in the Philebus (31d-32b) and Timaeus (64a-65b), where pleasure is restoration of the natural state after depletion. -- Aristotle's counterexamples — learning, smell, music, visual beauty, memory, hope — are chosen to show that the theory reduces all pleasure to the bodily and nutritive model. -- The pleasures of learning play a central role for Aristotle; cf. Metaphysics I.1 ("all men by nature desire to know") and Rhetoric I.11. -- This argument also connects to De Anima II-III on the different modalities of sense perception.

refashioned:
In reply to those who cite disgraceful pleasures as evidence against pleasure, one may respond that these are not truly pleasant. If things are pleasant to people of corrupt constitution, we need not assume they are pleasant to anyone else, just as we do not take what seems wholesome, sweet, or bitter to sick people as evidence of what is truly wholesome, sweet, or bitter, nor do we ascribe whiteness to things that appear white to those with diseased eyes.

Alternatively, one might respond that pleasures are indeed desirable, but not from every source — just as wealth is desirable but not as the reward for betrayal, and health is desirable but not at the cost of eating anything whatsoever. Or perhaps pleasures differ in kind: those derived from noble sources are genuinely different from those derived from base ones. One cannot experience the pleasure of the just person without being just, nor the pleasure of the musical person without being musical, and so on for the rest.
elegant: Disgraceful pleasures do not discredit pleasure as such. What seems pleasant to a corrupt person need not be truly pleasant, just as what tastes sweet to a sick person need not be truly sweet. Alternatively, pleasure is desirable but not from every source — just as wealth is desirable but not from betrayal. Or perhaps pleasures simply differ in kind: the pleasure of the just person requires being just, and the pleasure of the musician requires being musical.
summary: The existence of disgraceful pleasures does not prove that pleasure itself is bad. Either these are not truly pleasant but only seem so to corrupted people, or pleasures are desirable only from certain sources, or pleasures genuinely differ in kind — the pleasure of a just person can only be felt by someone who is just.
distilled: Disgraceful pleasures do not indict pleasure itself, since they may not be truly pleasant, may come from improper sources, or may simply be different in kind from noble pleasures.
notes: The argument from disgraceful pleasures was a standard objection in the Academy. -- Aristotle's response using the analogy of sick perception draws on his epistemology: the properly functioning perceiver is the standard of truth (cf. Metaphysics IV.5 on the truthfulness of normal perception). -- The idea that pleasures differ in kind is central to Aristotle's positive theory in X.5. -- The claim that one cannot feel the just person's pleasure without being just connects to II.3 on the role of pleasure in demonstrating genuine virtue: the truly virtuous person takes pleasure in virtuous action. -- This anticipates the "standard of the good person" argument in X.22.

refashioned:
The difference between a friend and a flatterer also makes it clear that pleasure is not the good, or that pleasures differ in kind. The friend is thought to associate with us for the sake of our good, the flatterer for the sake of our pleasure, and the flatterer is reproached while the friend is praised, precisely because they associate with us for different purposes.

Moreover, no one would choose to live their entire life with the mind of a child, however much pleasure they might take in childish things; nor would anyone choose to enjoy some utterly disgraceful act, even if no pain ever followed from it. And there are many things we would eagerly pursue even if they brought no pleasure at all — seeing, remembering, knowing, possessing the virtues. If pleasures necessarily accompany these, that makes no difference; we would choose them even if no pleasure resulted.

It seems clear, then, that pleasure is neither the good nor that all pleasure is desirable, but that some pleasures are desirable in themselves, differing in kind or in their sources from others. Let this suffice for our review of what has been said about pleasure and pain.
elegant:
The contrast between a friend and a flatterer shows either that pleasure is not the good, or that pleasures differ in kind: one associates with us for our good, the other for our pleasure. No one would choose a lifetime of childish pleasure or enjoy a disgraceful act without consequence. And we would pursue seeing, knowing, remembering, and possessing virtue even if no pleasure came with them.

Pleasure is therefore neither the good nor universally desirable; some pleasures are worthwhile in themselves, differing from others in kind or source.
summary: The distinction between friends and flatterers, the absurdity of choosing a lifetime of childish pleasure, and our willingness to pursue knowledge and virtue even without pleasure all demonstrate that pleasure is not the supreme good. Still, some pleasures are genuinely desirable in themselves, differing from inferior pleasures in kind or source.
distilled: We would choose seeing, knowing, and virtue even without pleasure, proving pleasure is not the supreme good, though some pleasures are intrinsically worthwhile.
notes: The friend-flatterer distinction connects to the extensive treatment of friendship in Books VIII-IX, especially VIII.3-4 on the three types of friendship (utility, pleasure, virtue). -- The thought experiment about living with a child's mind parallels the famous "experience machine" argument later formulated by Robert Nozick (Anarchy, State, and Utopia, 1974), challenging hedonism by showing we value more than subjective experience. -- The claim that seeing, remembering, and knowing are intrinsically valuable connects to Metaphysics I.1 and to Aristotle's broader commitment to theoria as the highest human good, which he develops in X.7-8. -- The conclusion that pleasures differ in kind is the bridge to Aristotle's own positive theory of pleasure beginning in X.4.

refashioned: What pleasure is, or what kind of thing it is, will become clearer if we take up the question again from the beginning. Seeing seems to be complete at every moment: it does not lack anything that would come into being later to complete its form. Pleasure too seems to be of this nature. It is a whole, and at no point can one find a pleasure whose form would be completed by the pleasure lasting longer. For this reason, too, pleasure is not a motion. Every motion — building, for example — takes time and is directed toward an end, and is complete only when it has achieved what it aims at. A motion is complete only in the whole time it takes, or at the final moment. In their parts and during the time they occupy, all motions are incomplete and differ in kind both from the whole motion and from each other.
elegant: Pleasure, like seeing, is complete at every moment — it does not become more complete by lasting longer. This distinguishes it fundamentally from motion. Every motion, such as building, takes time, aims at an end beyond itself, and is complete only in its entirety or at the final moment. Its parts are incomplete and differ in kind from the whole. Pleasure has none of these features.
summary: Aristotle begins his positive account of pleasure by arguing that pleasure, like the act of seeing, is complete at every moment rather than building toward completion over time. This distinguishes pleasure fundamentally from motion, which is always incomplete until it reaches its end.
distilled: Pleasure is complete at every instant, unlike motion, which is inherently incomplete until it achieves its end.
notes: This passage marks the beginning of Aristotle's own positive theory of pleasure, after the critical review of others' views in X.1-5. -- The distinction between energeia (activity, complete at every moment) and kinesis (motion, incomplete until reaching its goal) is one of Aristotle's most important philosophical contributions, developed in Metaphysics IX.6 (1048b18-35). -- The example of seeing as an activity complete at every moment is Aristotle's paradigm case of energeia in the Metaphysics. -- This analysis supersedes and refines the earlier treatment of pleasure in Book VII.11-14, which was more provisional. -- The contrast between pleasure and motion/process has been influential in philosophy of action, particularly in distinguishing activities from performances (Gilbert Ryle, Anthony Kenny).

refashioned: For the fitting together of stones is different from the fluting of a column, and both are different from the construction of the temple as a whole. The construction of the temple is complete — it lacks nothing relative to its intended end — but the making of the base or the triglyph is incomplete, since each is only the making of a part. These sub-processes differ in kind from one another, and it is impossible to find at any arbitrary moment a motion that is complete in form; if at all, only in the whole span of time. The same holds for walking and all other motions.
elegant: Fitting stones, fluting columns, and building the whole temple are different in kind. The temple's construction is complete only when finished; building a base or triglyph is inherently incomplete, being only part of the whole. No motion can be found complete at an arbitrary moment — only in its full duration.
summary: The sub-processes of building — fitting stones, carving details, constructing the whole — differ in kind from each other and are each incomplete until the whole project is finished, illustrating that motion is never complete at an arbitrary moment.
distilled: The parts of a building process differ in kind from each other and from the whole, and none is complete until the entire project reaches its end.
notes: The architectural examples (base, triglyph, column) are drawn from Greek temple construction, reflecting Aristotle's familiarity with the building projects of 4th-century Athens. -- A triglyph is a three-grooved block in a Doric frieze, a characteristic element of Greek temple architecture. -- This concrete illustration of kinesis being incomplete in its parts reinforces the abstract distinction between kinesis and energeia from Metaphysics IX.6. -- The point that parts of a motion differ in kind from the whole is crucial: a half-completed building is not a building in any complete sense.

refashioned:
If locomotion is a motion from one place to another, it too has differences in kind — flying, walking, leaping, and so on. And not only that: within walking itself there are such differences. The starting point and destination are not the same for the whole racecourse as for a part of it, nor the same in one part as in another. Nor is traversing one line the same as traversing another, since one traverses not merely a line in the abstract but a line in a particular place, and one stretch of ground is in a different place from another.

We have discussed motion with precision elsewhere, but it appears that motion is never complete at any arbitrary moment. Rather, the many constituent motions are incomplete and different in kind from one another, since their starting points and endpoints give them their specific character.
elegant: Even walking, a single type of locomotion, contains internal differences: traversing the first part of a racecourse differs from traversing the last, since each segment has different starting and ending points that give it its specific character. Motion is never complete at an arbitrary moment; its many phases are incomplete and differ in kind from one another.
summary: Even within a single type of motion like walking, each segment differs in kind because the starting and ending points differ. This confirms that motion is inherently incomplete at any given moment, with its parts being different in kind from each other and from the whole.
distilled: Each segment of a motion differs in kind from every other, since the specific character of any motion is determined by its starting and ending points.
notes: The reference to having "discussed motion with precision in another work" points to the Physics, particularly Books III, V, and VI, where Aristotle analyzes motion (kinesis), continuity, and the infinite divisibility of spatial magnitudes. -- The racecourse example is vivid: Greek athletic stadiums were common reference points. -- The point that the whence (pothen) and whither (poi) define the character of a motion connects to Physics V.1, where changes are classified by their termini. -- This analysis supports the broader metaphysical distinction between processes (kinesis) and activities (energeia) that is foundational to Aristotle's theory of pleasure.

refashioned: But the form of pleasure is complete at any and every moment. Plainly, then, pleasure and motion must be different from each other, and pleasure must be one of the things that are whole and complete. This would also seem to follow from the fact that motion cannot occur except in time, whereas it is possible to be pleased in a moment — for what takes place in an instant is a whole.
elegant: Pleasure's form is complete at every moment, making it fundamentally different from motion. Motion requires time and is always incomplete in its parts; pleasure can occur in an instant and is always a whole.
summary: Since pleasure is complete at every moment while motion is always incomplete until finished, pleasure must be categorically different from motion and must belong to the class of things that are whole and complete.
distilled: Pleasure is complete at every instant and therefore belongs to the category of wholes, not to the category of motions.
notes: This is the culmination of Aristotle's argument distinguishing pleasure from kinesis. The claim that pleasure is complete at every moment (teleion en hōtōioun chronōi) identifies pleasure as an energeia in the strict sense of Metaphysics IX.6. -- The temporal criterion — motion requires extended time while pleasure can be instantaneous — is a key differentiator. -- This has important implications: if pleasure is an activity rather than a process, it cannot be identified with bodily replenishment or any kind of becoming, which are processes by nature. -- J.L. Ackrill and others have debated whether Aristotle's energeia/kinesis distinction is primarily temporal or teleological.

refashioned: From these considerations it is also clear that those thinkers are wrong to speak of a motion or a coming-into-being of pleasure. Motion and coming-into-being can only be attributed to things that are divisible and not wholes. There is no coming-into-being of seeing, nor of a point, nor of a unit, and none of these is a motion or coming-into-being. Therefore there is no motion or coming-into-being of pleasure either, since pleasure is a whole.
elegant: Since motion and coming-into-being belong only to divisible things that are not wholes, and pleasure — like seeing, a point, or a unit — is a whole and indivisible, pleasure cannot be a motion or coming-into-being.
summary: Motion and coming-into-being can only be ascribed to divisible things, not to wholes. Since pleasure, like seeing or a geometric point, is complete and indivisible at every moment, it cannot be classified as either a motion or a coming-into-being.
distilled: Pleasure, being whole and indivisible like an act of seeing, is categorically excluded from the class of motions and processes of becoming.
notes: This passage concludes the negative argument against pleasure-as-kinesis and pleasure-as-genesis. -- The analogy with points and units draws on Aristotle's philosophy of mathematics; a point has no parts and thus cannot undergo a process of becoming (cf. Metaphysics V.6, XIII.2). -- The comparison of pleasure with seeing reinforces Aristotle's paradigm: seeing is the canonical example of energeia in Metaphysics IX.6 ("one sees and at the same time has seen"). -- Having disposed of these rival theories, Aristotle now turns to his positive account of how pleasure relates to activity.

refashioned:
Since every sense is active in relation to its object, and a sense in good condition acts perfectly in relation to the finest of its objects (for perfect activity seems to be of this nature — whether we attribute it to the sense itself or to the organ in which it resides makes no difference for our purposes), it follows that in each case the best activity is that of the best-conditioned organ in relation to the most excellent of its objects. And this activity will be the most complete and pleasant.

For there is pleasure attending every sense, and no less attending thought and contemplation. The most complete activity is the most pleasant, and the most complete is that of a well-conditioned faculty in relation to the worthiest of its objects. Pleasure completes the activity — though not in the same way as the combination of a fine object and a sound sense does. Just as health and the doctor are not causes of a person's being healthy in the same way, so too pleasure and the object-sense combination do not complete the activity in the same way.

That pleasure is produced in connection with each sense is clear, since we speak of sights and sounds as pleasant. It is also clear that pleasure arises most of all when the sense is at its best and is directed at an object that matches it. When both object and perceiver are at their best, pleasure will always be present, since the active and passive factors are both there.

Pleasure completes the activity not as the inherent state or disposition does, by being internal to it, but as a supervening end — like the bloom of youth on those in their prime. So long as the object of intellect or perception and the faculty of judgment or contemplation are as they should be, pleasure will be involved in the activity. For when the active and passive factors are unchanged and in the right relation, the same result naturally follows.
elegant:
Each sense achieves its best activity when in good condition and directed at its finest object, and this optimal activity is the most complete and pleasant. Pleasure accompanies every form of perception and thought, arising most fully when faculty and object are both at their best.

Pleasure completes the activity — not as an inherent state like a disposition, but as something that supervenes upon it, the way the bloom of youth crowns those in their prime. Whenever perceiver and perceived, or thinker and object of thought, are both in excellent condition and properly related, pleasure naturally follows.
summary: Pleasure arises from and completes every well-functioning activity of sense or thought. The best pleasure accompanies the best activity — when a faculty in peak condition engages with the finest object. Pleasure does not complete activity as an internal disposition does, but supervenes upon it like the bloom that crowns youth.
distilled: Pleasure is the natural bloom that supervenes on any activity when both the faculty and its object are functioning at their best.
notes: The image of pleasure as a bloom (hoion tois akmaiois he hora) supervening on activity is one of the most famous in Aristotle's corpus. -- The distinction between pleasure completing activity and the way a disposition (hexis) completes it is subtle: a hexis is internal to the possessor, while pleasure comes as an additional perfection from outside, so to speak. -- This connects to De Anima II.5 on the relationship between sense faculties and their objects, and to III.4-5 on intellect and its objects. -- The agent-patient (active-passive) framework reflects Aristotle's general metaphysics of potentiality and actuality. -- Aquinas developed this account extensively in Summa Theologiae I-II, Q.31-34, using Aristotle's analysis as the basis for his own theory of pleasure (delectatio).

refashioned: How is it, then, that no one experiences pleasure continuously? Is it not because we grow weary? All human beings are incapable of continuous activity, and since pleasure accompanies activity, pleasure too cannot be continuous. Some things delight us when they are new but later do so less, for the same reason. At first the mind is stimulated and intensely active about them, as when people stare hard at something, but afterward the activity relaxes and becomes slack, and the pleasure is dulled along with it.
elegant: No one can be pleased continuously because no one can be active continuously. When a thing is new, the mind is intensely engaged and the pleasure is vivid; as the activity relaxes through fatigue, the pleasure fades with it.
summary: Pleasure cannot be continuous because human activity cannot be continuous. Novelty stimulates intense mental engagement and vivid pleasure, but as our activity slackens through fatigue, the accompanying pleasure fades proportionally.
distilled: Pleasure fades not because it is inherently fleeting but because the activity it accompanies inevitably tires.
notes: This passage explains the waning of pleasure through the waning of activity, consistent with Aristotle's view that pleasure supervenes on activity rather than existing independently. -- The observation about novelty connects to Rhetoric I.11, where Aristotle discusses why new things are pleasant. -- The analysis anticipates modern psychological research on hedonic adaptation (the "hedonic treadmill"), though Aristotle's explanation is specifically tied to cognitive and perceptual fatigue rather than a general adaptation mechanism. -- The inability to sustain continuous activity is important for the argument in X.7-8 about contemplation being the most continuous human activity.

refashioned:
One might think that all people desire pleasure because they all aim at life. Life is an activity, and each person is active about those things and with those faculties that they love most — the musician with hearing in relation to melodies, the student with their mind in relation to theoretical questions, and so on in each case. Pleasure completes these activities, and therefore completes life, which is what people desire. It is with good reason, then, that they also aim at pleasure, since for each person it completes that life which they find desirable.

Whether we choose life for the sake of pleasure or pleasure for the sake of life is a question we may set aside for now. The two seem to be bound up together and not to admit of separation, since without activity pleasure does not arise, and every activity is completed by the pleasure that attends it.
elegant: All people desire pleasure because they desire life, and life is activity. Each person is most active in what they love most, and pleasure completes that activity. Pleasure and life are so intertwined as to be inseparable: without activity there is no pleasure, and every activity is completed by its attendant pleasure.
summary: People desire pleasure because they desire life, and life is activity. Pleasure completes whatever activities we love most — music, study, and so on — and thereby completes life itself. Pleasure and life are inseparable, since there is no pleasure without activity and no activity reaches its fullness without pleasure.
distilled: Pleasure and life are inseparable because pleasure completes the activities that constitute life, and without activity there is no pleasure.
notes: The identification of life with activity (energeia) is foundational for Aristotle; cf. De Anima II.1, where the soul is defined as the first actuality of a natural body with the potential for life. -- The examples of the musician and the student illustrate the diversity of human lives and the corresponding diversity of pleasures, leading into the argument of X.5. -- The question of whether we choose life for pleasure or pleasure for life and the conclusion that they are inseparable anticipates the close connection Aristotle draws between the best activity and the best pleasure in X.7-8. -- This passage is important for understanding why Aristotle, unlike the Stoics, gives pleasure a central and positive role in the good life rather than treating it as indifferent.

refashioned: For this reason, pleasures also seem to differ in kind. Things that differ in kind are, we think, completed by different things — this is true of both natural objects and artifacts such as animals, trees, paintings, sculptures, houses, and tools. Similarly, activities that differ in kind are completed by things that differ in kind. Now the activities of thought differ from those of the senses, and both differ among themselves, in kind. Therefore the pleasures that complete them also differ in kind.
elegant: Since things different in kind are completed by different things — as we see with animals, artworks, and tools — and since the activities of thought differ in kind from those of the senses, and both differ among themselves, the pleasures that complete these activities must also differ in kind.
summary: Just as different kinds of things are completed by different kinds of perfections, so different kinds of activities — intellectual and sensory, and the varieties within each — are completed by different kinds of pleasure. Pleasures therefore differ in kind, not merely in degree.
distilled: Pleasures differ in kind because the activities they complete differ in kind, just as different kinds of things require different kinds of perfection.
notes: This is the key thesis of X.5: pleasures are not homogeneous but differ in kind (eidei) according to the activities they complete. This directly contradicts utilitarian and hedonistic assumptions that pleasures can be measured on a single scale. -- The argument from analogy (natural objects and artifacts require different completions) draws on the four-cause framework of Physics II and Metaphysics. -- This position has significant ethical implications: if pleasures differ in kind, then the pleasure of contemplation is not merely more pleasure than bodily enjoyment, but a fundamentally different kind of pleasure. -- J.S. Mill's distinction between "higher" and "lower" pleasures in Utilitarianism (1863) is sometimes seen as an attempt to incorporate an Aristotelian insight into a utilitarian framework.

refashioned: This can also be seen from the fact that each pleasure is bound up with the activity it completes. An activity is intensified by its proper pleasure, since people who engage in an activity with pleasure judge and grasp its details better. Those who enjoy geometry, for instance, become better geometers and understand the various propositions more deeply. Similarly, those who love music or architecture make greater progress in their respective pursuits by taking pleasure in them. The pleasures intensify the activities, and what intensifies a thing is proper to it. But things different in kind have properties different in kind.
elegant: Each pleasure is bound to the activity it completes, and intensifies it: those who take pleasure in geometry become better geometers, and those who love music advance further in music. Since what intensifies a thing is proper to it, and things different in kind have different proper properties, pleasures that enhance different activities must themselves differ in kind.
summary: Pleasure intensifies the activity it accompanies: people who enjoy geometry become better geometers, and musicians who love their art progress further. Since what intensifies an activity must be proper to it, and different activities have different proper properties, pleasures must differ in kind.
distilled: The right pleasure does not merely accompany its activity but actively intensifies and perfects it, which is why geometers who enjoy geometry become better geometers.
notes: The principle that pleasure intensifies its proper activity has significant pedagogical implications and connects to Aristotle's views on education in the Politics (VIII.3-7). -- The examples of geometry and music as pleasurable learning echo the Metaphysics I.1 claim that learning is naturally pleasant. -- This argument provides empirical support for the thesis that pleasures differ in kind: the pleasure of geometry specifically improves geometric ability, not musical ability. -- The concept of "proper pleasure" (oikeia hedone) is central: each activity has a pleasure native to it that enhances performance. -- This insight resonates with modern psychology's concept of "flow" (Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi), where intrinsic enjoyment of an activity correlates with optimal performance.

refashioned:
This becomes even more apparent from the fact that activities are hindered by pleasures arising from other sources. People who love flute-playing cannot pay attention to an argument if they overhear someone playing the flute, since they enjoy the flute-playing more than the activity at hand. The pleasure connected with flute-playing thus destroys the activity of reasoning. The same thing happens in all other cases when someone is engaged in two activities at once: the more pleasant activity drives out the other, and the more pleasant it is, the more completely it does so, until one ceases the other activity entirely. This is why when we enjoy something very much, we do not throw ourselves into anything else, and we engage in a second activity only when we are not much pleased by the first — as when people in the theater eat sweets most when the actors are poor.

Since activities are made more precise, more enduring, and better by their proper pleasure, but damaged by alien pleasures, it is clear that the two kinds of pleasure are far apart. Alien pleasures do essentially what proper pains do: activities are destroyed by the pains that belong to them. If someone finds writing or calculating unpleasant and painful, they do not write or calculate, because the activity is painful. An activity thus suffers contrary effects from its proper pleasures and its proper pains — that is, from those that supervene on it by virtue of its own nature. And alien pleasures have been shown to do much the same as pain: they destroy the activity, though not quite to the same degree.
elegant:
Activities are hindered by pleasures from alien sources. Flute-lovers cannot concentrate on arguments when they hear flute music; theater-goers eat sweets when the acting is poor. The more pleasant the competing activity, the more completely it displaces the other.

Proper pleasure enhances an activity, making it more precise, enduring, and excellent. Alien pleasure undermines it, much as the activity's own proper pain would — though not quite as destructively. This stark contrast between proper and alien pleasures confirms that pleasures differ fundamentally in kind.
summary: Pleasures from unrelated activities interfere with and can destroy the activity at hand, just as pain does. The pleasure of flute music disrupts argument; poor performances drive audiences to eat sweets. By contrast, an activity's proper pleasure enhances it. This opposition between proper and alien pleasures confirms they differ fundamentally in kind.
distilled: An activity's own pleasure perfects it while alien pleasures destroy it, proving that pleasures differ not just in degree but in kind.
notes: The flute-playing example is characteristically vivid and drawn from everyday Athenian life. The theater example — eating sweets during bad performances — is a wonderfully observed detail of audience behavior. -- The concept of alien (allotria) vs. proper (oikeia) pleasure is the practical corollary of the thesis that pleasures differ in kind. -- The parallel between alien pleasure and proper pain is striking: both undermine activity, though alien pleasure does so less severely. -- This analysis has implications for contemporary discussions of distraction, multitasking, and attention: Aristotle would view the inability to resist competing pleasures as a structural feature of how pleasure and attention interact, not merely a matter of willpower. -- The passage also connects to the discussion of akrasia (weakness of will) in Book VII.

refashioned:
Now since activities differ in goodness and badness — some being worthy of choice, others of avoidance, and others neutral — so too are their pleasures. The pleasure proper to a worthy activity is good, and the pleasure proper to an unworthy activity is bad, just as appetites for noble objects are praiseworthy while appetites for base objects are blameworthy.

The pleasures involved in activities are more closely connected to them than the desires that precede them. Desires are separated from their activities both in time and in nature, while pleasures are so close to the activities that it is difficult to distinguish them, leading some to wonder whether the activity might simply be the same thing as the pleasure. Still, pleasure does not seem to be identical with thinking or perceiving — that would be strange. But because they are never found apart, they appear to some people to be the same.

As activities differ, then, so do their corresponding pleasures. Sight is purer than touch, and hearing and smell are purer than taste. The pleasures are therefore similarly ranked, and the pleasures of thought are superior to all of these, with further hierarchies within each of the two groups — sensory and intellectual.
elegant:
Since activities differ in worth, their pleasures do too: the pleasure of a worthy activity is good, that of an unworthy one bad. Pleasures are so intimately connected to their activities that some wonder whether they are identical — though identifying pleasure with thinking or perceiving would be strange.

A hierarchy of pleasures mirrors the hierarchy of activities: sight's pleasures are purer than touch's, hearing's and smell's purer than taste's, and the pleasures of thought surpass all sensory pleasures, with further gradations within each domain.
summary: Pleasures inherit the moral quality of their activities: good activities produce good pleasures, bad activities bad ones. Because pleasure is so intimately tied to activity, a hierarchy of pleasures follows the hierarchy of senses and faculties — with intellectual pleasures ranking highest, followed by the purer senses like sight and hearing over touch and taste.
distilled: Pleasures rank in the same hierarchy as the activities they complete, with intellectual pleasures highest and touch-pleasures lowest.
notes: The ranking of senses — sight above hearing, hearing above taste, touch lowest — reflects Aristotle's analysis in De Anima II.7-11 and his conviction that the senses differ in precision and remove from bodily contact. -- The near-identification of pleasure with activity that Aristotle entertains and then rejects is philosophically significant: it shows how close his view comes to making pleasure constitutive of (rather than merely attendant upon) activity. -- The moral valuation of pleasures by the moral quality of their activities is central to Aristotle's ethical framework and distinguishes his position from both hedonism (which values all pleasure) and anti-hedonism (which suspects all pleasure). -- Aquinas adopted this hierarchy in his discussion of delectatio in the Summa Theologiae.

refashioned:
Each animal is thought to have its own proper pleasure, just as it has its own proper function — namely, the pleasure that corresponds to its characteristic activity. This becomes evident when we survey animals species by species: horse, dog, and human being have different pleasures. As Heraclitus says, "asses would prefer sweepings to gold," for food is more pleasant than gold to an ass.

So the pleasures of creatures different in kind differ in kind, and it would be reasonable to suppose that those within a single species do not differ. Yet they vary to no small extent, at least among human beings. The same things delight some people and cause pain to others; what is painful and repulsive to some is pleasant and attractive to others. This also happens with sweet things: the same foods do not taste sweet to someone with a fever and to a healthy person, nor does the same thing feel hot to a weak person and to one in good condition.

But in all such matters, what appears so to the good person is taken to be truly so. If this is correct, as it seems to be, and if virtue and the good person as such are the measure of each thing, then those will be genuine pleasures which appear as pleasures to the good person, and those things will be truly pleasant which they enjoy. If things the good person finds tiresome seem pleasant to someone else, that is not surprising, since human beings can be corrupted and spoiled in many ways. Such things are not truly pleasant — only pleasant to these particular people in their particular condition.

As for admittedly disgraceful pleasures, plainly they should not be called pleasures at all, except from the perspective of a corrupted sensibility. But among pleasures thought to be good, which kind, or which particular pleasure, should be called the pleasure proper to a human being? Is it not evident from the corresponding activities? The pleasures follow from these. Whether, then, the fully good and supremely happy person has one or more activities, the pleasures that complete these activities will be called pleasures proper to a human being in the truest sense, while the rest will be so only in a secondary and diminished way, just as their activities are.
elegant:
Each animal has a proper pleasure corresponding to its proper function, and species differ: as Heraclitus said, asses prefer sweepings to gold. Even within the human species, pleasures vary enormously — what delights one person pains another.

The standard is the good person. What appears pleasant to the virtuous person is truly pleasant; if others disagree, it is because corruption distorts their perception. Disgraceful pleasures are not genuinely pleasures at all. The pleasures proper to human beings are those that complete the activities of the fully virtuous and happy person; all others are pleasures only in a secondary sense.
summary: Different species have different proper pleasures corresponding to their different functions. Even among humans, tastes vary widely. The good person serves as the standard of what is truly pleasant: genuine human pleasures are those that complete the activities of the virtuous person, while all others are pleasures only in a degraded sense.
distilled: The good person is the measure of true pleasure, and the pleasures proper to human beings are those that complete the activities of the virtuous.
notes: Heraclitus of Ephesus (c. 535-475 BCE), the pre-Socratic philosopher known for doctrines of flux and the unity of opposites. The fragment about asses preferring sweepings to gold (DK B9) illustrates the relativity of value across different natures. -- The principle that the good person (spoudaios) is the measure (metron) of what is truly pleasant and truly good is one of Aristotle's most distinctive ethical claims, connecting to his treatment of the practically wise person (phronimos) in Book VI. -- This echoes Protagoras's "man is the measure" doctrine while transforming it: not any person, but the virtuous person, is the standard. -- The argument that corrupted people perceive falsely parallels Aristotle's epistemology of normal vs. diseased perception in Metaphysics IV.5. -- This passage brings the discussion of pleasure full circle back to the question of happiness, preparing for the famous argument of X.6-8.

refashioned:
Now that we have discussed the virtues, the forms of friendship, and the varieties of pleasure, what remains is to give an outline account of happiness, since this is what we identified as the end of human nature. Our discussion will be more concise if we first summarize what we have already established.

We said that happiness is not a disposition. If it were, it could belong to someone asleep throughout their entire life, living the life of a plant, or to someone enduring the worst misfortunes. If these implications are unacceptable, and we must instead classify happiness as an activity — as we have said before — and if some activities are merely necessary or desirable as means to other things while others are desirable in themselves, then clearly happiness must be placed among things desirable in themselves, not among those desired for the sake of something else. Happiness lacks nothing and is self-sufficient.

Activities desirable in themselves are those from which nothing is sought beyond the activity itself. Virtuous actions are thought to be of this nature, for doing noble and good deeds is something desirable for its own sake.
elegant: Having treated virtue, friendship, and pleasure, we now turn to happiness itself. Happiness cannot be a mere disposition — otherwise the sleeping, the vegetative, and the wretched could be called happy. It must be an activity, and specifically one desirable in itself, not as a means to anything else. It is self-sufficient, lacking nothing. Virtuous actions qualify as such activities, since doing noble deeds is desirable for its own sake.
summary: Aristotle recapitulates his earlier conclusions to begin his final account of happiness. Happiness cannot be a passive state or disposition but must be an activity desirable in itself, self-sufficient and complete. Virtuous actions qualify because they are pursued for their own sake.
distilled: Happiness is not a mere disposition but a self-sufficient activity desirable for its own sake, of which virtuous action is the clearest example.
notes: This passage opens the climactic argument of the Ethics (X.6-8) by recalling conclusions from Book I, especially I.7 (the function argument) and I.8-10 (happiness as activity, not disposition). -- The "sleeping throughout life" and "life of a plant" thought experiments appeared in I.5 and I.10. -- The criterion of self-sufficiency (autarkeia) was established in I.7. -- The summary method here is characteristic of Aristotle's dialectical approach: he reviews endoxa (reputable opinions) and established conclusions before advancing to new ground. -- The argument that follows in X.7-8, identifying contemplation as the highest happiness, has been among the most debated passages in the history of philosophy.

refashioned:
Pleasant amusements might also seem to be activities desirable in themselves, since we do not choose them for the sake of other things. In fact, we are harmed rather than helped by them, since they lead us to neglect our bodies and our property. Yet most people who are reputed to be happy take refuge in such pastimes, which is why those who are witty and entertaining are highly valued at the courts of tyrants: they make themselves agreeable companions in the tyrants' favored diversions, and that is the sort of person tyrants want. These amusements seem to constitute happiness because people in positions of power spend their leisure on them.

But such people prove nothing. Virtue and reason, the sources of good activities, do not depend on holding power. And if these people, who have never experienced pure and noble pleasure, resort to bodily pleasures, we should not on that account think bodily pleasures more desirable. Children, too, think the things valued among children are the best. It is to be expected, then, that just as different things seem valuable to children and to adults, so they should to bad people and to good people.

As we have often maintained, the things that are truly valuable and pleasant are those that appear so to the good person. For each person, the most desirable activity is the one that accords with their own character, and for the good person, this means the activity that accords with virtue. Happiness, therefore, does not consist in amusement. It would indeed be absurd if the end of life were amusement, and one labored and suffered hardship all one's life in order to amuse oneself. Everything we choose, we choose for the sake of something else — except happiness, which is an end. To exert oneself and toil for the sake of amusement seems foolish and utterly childish. But to amuse oneself in order to exert oneself afterward, as Anacharsis puts it, seems right. For amusement is a form of relaxation, and we need relaxation because we cannot work continuously. Relaxation, then, is not an end; it is pursued for the sake of activity.
elegant:
Amusement might seem to be an end in itself, since we do not pursue it for anything further, and powerful people spend their leisure on it. But this proves nothing: power does not bring virtue, and those who have never tasted noble pleasure naturally gravitate toward bodily pleasure, just as children value childish things.

Happiness cannot consist in amusement. It would be absurd to toil all one's life for the sake of play. Amusement is relaxation, and relaxation exists for the sake of renewed activity, not the reverse. As Anacharsis said, we play in order to work, not the other way around. Everything is chosen for the sake of something else — except happiness, which is the end.
summary: Amusement cannot constitute happiness, even though powerful people spend their leisure on it, because power does not confer virtue or discernment. It would be absurd to labor all one's life merely to amuse oneself. Amusement is relaxation, which serves activity rather than being an end in itself.
distilled: We amuse ourselves in order to work, not the other way around, so amusement cannot be what happiness consists in.
notes: Anacharsis was a legendary Scythian sage (6th century BCE), one of the Seven Sages in some ancient lists, known for pithy observations about Greek customs. -- The critique of tyrants' courts reflects Aristotle's political experience; he spent time at the Macedonian court and was tutor to Alexander the Great. -- The argument that powerful people's choices do not establish what is truly good is a pointed social observation: wealth and power do not guarantee wisdom about the good life. -- The children analogy recurs from I.3, where Aristotle warns that the young are not proper students of ethics. -- The principle that relaxation serves activity (and not vice versa) is important for the Politics (VIII.3-5), where Aristotle discusses the proper use of leisure (schole) in education.

refashioned:
The happy life is thought to be a life of virtue, and a virtuous life requires serious effort rather than consisting in amusement. We hold that serious things are better than laughable things and those connected with amusement, and that the activity of the better part — whether we are comparing two parts of our being or two people — is the more serious. But the activity of what is better is by that very fact superior and more characteristic of happiness.

Anyone at all — even a slave — can enjoy bodily pleasures no less than the finest person. But no one assigns a share in happiness to a slave — unless they also assign to a slave a share in truly human life. For happiness does not consist in such pastimes but, as we have said before, in activities of virtue.
elegant: The happy life requires virtue and serious effort, not amusement. Serious things surpass laughable ones, and the activity of the better part of our nature is more characteristic of happiness. Anyone, even a slave, can enjoy bodily pleasures equally — but no one attributes happiness to a slave who does not share in a truly human life. Happiness lies in virtuous activity, not in pastimes.
summary: Happiness requires the serious exercise of virtue, not mere amusement. While anyone can enjoy bodily pleasures, happiness is not attributed to those who lack a share in genuinely human life. The activity of our higher nature is inherently more connected to happiness than passive enjoyment.
distilled: Happiness requires the serious exercise of virtue, not amusement, since even a slave can enjoy bodily pleasures but is not thereby called happy.
notes: The slave example reflects ancient Greek social reality but carries a philosophical point: Aristotle is arguing that bodily pleasure is not sufficient for happiness because it does not engage what is distinctively human. -- Aristotle's view of slaves is complex and controversial; Politics I.4-7 contains his problematic defense of natural slavery, though some scholars argue his criteria would exclude most actual slaves. -- The distinction between serious (spoudaios) and laughable or trivial anticipates the hierarchy of activities in X.7. -- The claim that the better part's activity is more characteristic of happiness prepares for the argument that contemplation, as the activity of the highest part (nous), is the supreme form of happiness. -- This passage bridges the negative argument (happiness is not amusement) to the positive argument (happiness is contemplation) that follows.

refashioned: If happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, it is reasonable that it should be in accordance with the highest virtue, and that will be the virtue of the best thing in us. Whether this element is reason or something else — whatever it is that is thought to be our natural ruler and guide, that takes thought of things noble and divine — and whether it is itself divine or merely the most divine element in us, the activity of this element in accordance with its proper virtue will be perfect happiness. That this activity is contemplation we have already said.
elegant: If happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, it must accord with the highest virtue — that of the best thing in us. Whatever this ruling element is, whether reason or something beyond it, whether itself divine or merely the most divine part of our nature, its proper activity is contemplation, and this constitutes perfect happiness.
summary: Since happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, the highest happiness must consist in the activity of our highest faculty — reason, our natural ruler, which grasps what is noble and divine. This supreme activity is contemplation.
distilled: Perfect happiness is the contemplative activity of reason, the highest and most divine element in human nature.
notes: This pivotal passage announces the thesis of X.7-8: perfect happiness (teleia eudaimonia) is contemplation (theoria). -- The identification of reason (nous) as the ruling element connects to De Anima III.4-5 and the Protrepticus (a lost work partially reconstructable from quotations). -- The tentative phrasing ("whether reason or something else") may reflect genuine philosophical uncertainty or dialectical caution about identifying nous with the divine. -- The reference to having "already said" this points back to I.7 (the function argument) and possibly to the discussion of sophia (theoretical wisdom) in VI.7. -- This identification of happiness with contemplation is the most controversial claim in Aristotle's ethics, leading to the so-called "dominant" vs. "inclusive" debate about eudaimonia: does Aristotle mean that contemplation alone constitutes happiness, or that it is the highest component of a life that also includes practical virtue?

refashioned: This would seem to agree with what we said before and with the truth. First, this activity is the best, since reason is the best thing in us, and the objects of reason are the best among knowable things. Second, it is the most continuous, since we can sustain contemplation of truth more continuously than we can sustain any other activity. Third, we consider happiness to have pleasure mixed in with it, and the activity of philosophic wisdom is acknowledged to be the most pleasant of virtuous activities. The pursuit of philosophy is thought to offer pleasures of extraordinary purity and endurance, and it is to be expected that those who possess knowledge spend their time more pleasantly than those who are still seeking it. Furthermore, the self-sufficiency we speak of must belong most of all to the contemplative activity.
elegant: Contemplation satisfies all the criteria for perfect happiness. It is the best activity, since reason and its objects are the best in us. It is the most continuous, since we can sustain contemplation longer than any other pursuit. It is the most pleasant, for philosophy offers pleasures unmatched in purity and endurance. And it is the most self-sufficient of all activities.
summary: Contemplation meets all the requirements for the highest happiness: it exercises the best faculty (reason) on the best objects, it is the most continuous human activity, it provides the purest and most enduring pleasures, and it is the most self-sufficient pursuit.
distilled: Contemplation is the highest happiness because it is the best, most continuous, most pleasant, and most self-sufficient of all human activities.
notes: The four criteria applied here — best, most continuous, most pleasant, most self-sufficient — recall the criteria for happiness established in Book I.7. Aristotle is showing that contemplation satisfies all of them. -- The claim that contemplation is the most continuous activity connects to X.4's discussion of why pleasure fades (because activity tires), suggesting that contemplation tires least. -- The distinction between those who know and those who inquire (echontes vs. zetountes) reflects Aristotle's view that possessing knowledge is more pleasant than acquiring it, connecting to the energeia/kinesis distinction. -- The pleasure of philosophy "marvellous for purity and enduringness" is a rare moment of personal testimony from Aristotle. -- The self-sufficiency criterion becomes important in the next passage, where Aristotle compares contemplation with practical virtue.

refashioned:
For while a philosopher, like a just person or anyone possessing any other virtue, needs the necessities of life, once sufficiently equipped in that regard, the just person additionally needs other people toward whom and with whom to act justly, and the same is true of the temperate person, the brave person, and each of the others. The philosopher, however, can contemplate truth even when entirely alone, and the wiser one is, the better one can do so. It may be that having fellow inquirers makes it easier, but the philosopher is still the most self-sufficient of all.

Moreover, this activity alone seems to be loved purely for its own sake. Nothing comes from it beyond the contemplation itself, while from practical activities we always gain something more or less beyond the action. And happiness is thought to depend on leisure. We are busy so that we may have leisure, and we make war so that we may live in peace.
elegant: The philosopher, unlike the just, brave, or temperate person, does not need other people to exercise their highest activity. The philosopher can contemplate truth alone, and the wiser they are, the better they can do so — making contemplation the most self-sufficient activity. It is also loved purely for its own sake, since nothing results from it beyond the contemplating itself. And since happiness requires leisure, and we are busy precisely in order to have leisure, contemplation fits the requirement perfectly.
summary: Contemplation is the most self-sufficient activity because the philosopher can pursue it alone, unlike the practically virtuous person who needs others. It is also pursued purely for its own sake, producing nothing beyond itself. Since happiness requires leisure and all our busyness aims at leisure, contemplation — the supreme leisure activity — is best suited to be happiness.
distilled: Contemplation is the most self-sufficient activity because it requires no one else, produces nothing beyond itself, and is the supreme use of the leisure that all our busyness aims to secure.
notes: The argument about self-sufficiency marks a crucial contrast between practical and theoretical virtue. The just person needs others as objects and partners in justice; the contemplative needs no one, though companions help. -- The claim that happiness depends on leisure (schole) is foundational for Aristotle's political philosophy. In Politics VII-VIII, the well-ordered state exists to provide citizens with leisure for noble activities. -- The famous line "we are busy that we may have leisure, and make war that we may live in peace" (ascholoumetha hina scholazomen) inverts common priorities. -- This passage has been central to the contemplation-vs.-action debate in Western philosophy, influencing Hannah Arendt's distinction between vita activa and vita contemplativa in The Human Condition (1958). -- The Stoics would later disagree, arguing that virtue in practical life is sufficient for happiness.

refashioned:
The practical virtues find their exercise in political and military affairs, but actions in these domains are inherently unleisurely. Military actions are completely so — no one chooses to wage war or provokes it for the sake of being at war. Anyone who made enemies of friends in order to bring about battles and slaughter would seem absolutely murderous. The statesman's activity is also unleisurely and, beyond the political action itself, aims at something further — power, honors, or at least a happiness for the statesman and fellow citizens that is different from the political action and is evidently sought as something distinct from it.

So if among virtuous actions political and military deeds are distinguished by nobility and greatness, yet are unleisurely, aim at ends beyond themselves, and are not desirable for their own sake, while the activity of reason — which is contemplative — seems both superior in worth and to aim at no end beyond itself, and to have its own proper pleasure (which augments the activity), and to possess self-sufficiency, leisureliness, and freedom from weariness (so far as this is possible for a human being), and all the other attributes ascribed to the supremely happy person — then this must be the complete happiness of a human being, provided it is allowed a complete span of life. For none of the attributes of happiness is incomplete.
elegant:
Political and military action, though noble, are unleisurely and aim at ends beyond themselves — no one makes war for its own sake, and statesmanship seeks results distinct from the political activity itself. Contemplation, by contrast, is superior in worth, aims at nothing beyond itself, carries its own pleasure, and possesses self-sufficiency, leisureliness, and unwearied persistence.

Since contemplation alone exhibits all the attributes of complete happiness — given a full span of life — it must be the complete happiness of a human being.
summary: Political and military activities, though noble, are not pursued for their own sake and are inherently lacking in leisure. Contemplation alone combines superiority of worth, self-sufficiency, leisureliness, its own proper pleasure, and freedom from weariness — all the marks of the supremely happy life. It is therefore the complete happiness of human beings, given a full lifespan.
distilled: Contemplation alone unites all the attributes of complete happiness — self-sufficiency, leisureliness, intrinsic worth, proper pleasure, and unwearied persistence — that political and military action, however noble, lack.
